currency_system.philosophy: Steel is both apex currency and material resource. Hoarding money suppresses the weapons market. Wars consume currency literally. Currency and resource are the same substance.

tiers.name: Iron Chit
tiers.purchasing_power: A loaf of bread, a night's floor-sleeping, a cup of bad ale
tiers.notes: Used at village and laborer level

tiers.name: Bronze Mark
tiers.purchasing_power: A laborer's weekly wage. A cheap knife. Basic tool repair.

tiers.name: Steel Disc
tiers.purchasing_power: A sword. A month's mercenary contract. A horse. A skilled craftsman's monthly wage.
tiers.notes: Rare enough that receiving one is noteworthy. Can be melted into weapons — economic tension point.

economic_simulation_rules: All currency is finite — there is no infinite money source
Steel discs removed from circulation (melted, lost, buried) reduce weapons market supply
Wars consume steel currency — armies need weapons, weapons need steel
A background system manages total currency in circulation at all times
Prices fluctuate based on regional scarcity and demand
Coin hoarding by NPCs is modeled and affects local liquidity

economy_simulation.resource_loop: Resources are extracted, processed, traded, consumed, destroyed. No source is infinite. Scarcity is real and emergent.
economy_simulation.population_economy: Every NPC has needs (food, shelter, coin). Deaths remove people from the labor pool. War killing farmers → food price rises → unrest.
economy_simulation.seasonal_pressure: Winter is a resource crisis. Drought is a crisis. Plague is a crisis. These emerge from simulation state, not scripts.
economy_simulation.faction_economics: Lords, guilds, companies all have resources and pursue goals autonomously. Their economic decisions affect regional prices.
key_tracked_variables: Total currency in circulation by region
Food production vs consumption by region
Resource stock levels (iron ore, timber, grain, etc.)
